planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil has reached 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–April. Moon and Stars is a large, late-maturing heirloom requiring 90–100 days and vigorous vines — allow ample space (6–8 feet between hills). In zone 9's long warm season it reaches peak maturity with excellent sweetness.

directions: Sow 3–4 seeds per hill in well-amended, well-drained soil in full sun; Moon and Stars is one of the most visually distinctive watermelon heirlooms — the deep green, nearly black skin is decorated with golden yellow spots mimicking the moon and stars, with smaller 'stars' scattered across the leaves as well. The striking appearance made it a show-stopping heirloom that Seed Savers Exchange famously rescued from near extinction in 1981 when Merle Van Doren of Missouri was discovered growing the last known strain. The large oval fruits (15–40 pounds) have deep red, sweet flesh with good flavor. Allow vines to run freely 6–8 feet; thin to 2 plants per hill. Harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit withers and the skin resists fingernail pressure, and when the underside turns creamy yellow. This variety needs the long, hot growing season of zone 9 to develop its full size and sweetness. Seed Savers Exchange offers several strains including red and yellow-flesh selections.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Moon and Stars watermelon historical documentation
William Woys Weaver: Heirloom Vegetable Gardening, Henry Holt 1997
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is at least 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–April. Ali Baba is an Iraqi/Middle Eastern landrace watermelon specifically adapted to intense heat and dry conditions — an excellent choice for zone 9 where summer heat can stress ordinary watermelons.

directions: Plant in hills spaced 6–8 feet apart in full sun, well-drained, fertile soil; Ali Baba is an heirloom watermelon landrace from Iraq, where it has been cultivated for centuries in the intense heat and aridity of the Tigris-Euphrates region. The large (20–30 pound), pale green, blocky-oval fruits have vibrant red, exceptionally sweet and flavorful flesh with small seeds. What sets Ali Baba apart for Gulf Coast and zone 9 gardeners is its remarkable adaptation to heat: it continues setting fruit and maintains its characteristic sweetness through temperatures that stress modern commercial varieties, making it one of the best heirlooms for hot, dry or hot-humid summer conditions. It is also notably drought-tolerant compared to most watermelons once established. Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds introduced it to American growers and it has gained a devoted following among gardeners who prize heat tolerance and excellent flavor. Harvest when the tendril nearest the fruit dries and the skin lightens; the yellow ground spot also intensifies as fruit ripens.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Ali Baba watermelon variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange — Middle Eastern watermelon landrace documentation
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange
USDA National Plant Germplasm System — Citrullus lanatus accessions

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost when soil has reached 70°F; in zone 9 plant March–April for summer harvest, or August for fall. Crenshaw is a large melon requiring 90–110 days and very hot conditions for full ripening — best in zone 9 gardens with a long, warm summer. Requires 6–8 feet between plants.

directions: Plant in hills of 3–4 seeds in full sun, well-drained, fertile soil; Crenshaw (also spelled Cranshaw) is an old American hybrid between Casaba and Persian melon types, producing very large (8–10 pound), distinctively shaped fruits — pointed at the stem end, rounded at the blossom end, with smooth, pale green-yellow skin that turns golden-yellow at maturity. The flesh is thick, deep salmon-orange, nearly seed-free in the center, very juicy, extremely sweet and fragrant with a complex flavor combining the rich sweetness of cantaloupe with the floral aromatics of a Persian melon — widely considered among the finest-flavored melons available. Crenshaw requires intense heat to develop its characteristic sweetness, making zone 9's long summer ideal. Harvest when the blossom end yields to gentle thumb pressure and the skin turns golden and waxy; a fully ripe Crenshaw has a heavenly, heady fragrance. They do not keep long after picking — eat within a few days. For years they were a fixture at high-end produce markets and disappearing from supply chains has made them a garden treasure.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Crenshaw melon variety notes
Seed Savers Exchange
University of California Cooperative Extension — Specialty Melon Production
Amy Goldman: Melons for the Passionate Grower, Artisan 2002

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 start January–February and transplant February–March. Mulato is a full-season pepper requiring 80–100 days to first mature fruit — start early for best zone 9 production before summer heat peaks.

directions: Start seeds at 75–80°F and transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Mulato is a Mexican dried chile closely related to poblano but from a distinct gene pool (the seed color is tan, versus black for poblano/ancho) that produces heart-shaped, medium-large fruits, dark green turning dark brownish-black (rather than red) when fully ripe and dried. In dried form, mulato is one of the trio of chiles (together with ancho and pasilla) used in traditional Mexican mole negro sauce — its distinctive contribution is a dark, chocolate-tobacco, raisin-like complexity that makes mole negro deep and bittersweet. Heat level is mild to moderate (500–2,500 Scoville). Fresh mulato fruits are used in chiles rellenos and sauces; dried mulatos are rehydrated for mole and enchilada sauces. The plants are productive in zone 9's warm season and the fruits dry naturally on the plant in hot, dry conditions or can be dried in a dehydrator. An essential ingredient for serious Mexican cooking and home food preservers.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University — Mulato Pepper
Diana Kennedy: The Art of Mexican Cooking, Bantam Books 1989
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds
National Plant Germplasm System — Capsicum annuum accessions

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 sow October–February for fall and winter harvest. Fernleaf dill is a compact, bolt-resistant variety that stays in the leafy foliage stage 2–3 weeks longer than standard dill before bolting to flower. Ideal for container growing and small gardens.

directions: Scatter seeds thinly and press in lightly on the soil surface in full sun; Fernleaf is an All-America Selections winner dwarf dill variety that grows to only 18–24 inches (versus 4–5 feet for standard dill) with fine, feathery, dark blue-green foliage. The compact size makes it ideal for container growing, small herb gardens, and raised beds where standard dill would overwhelm its neighbors. It is also notably slower to bolt than standard varieties — where standard dill races to flower within 6 weeks, Fernleaf holds its leafy form for 8–10 weeks before setting flower heads. For zone 9 gardeners, this bolt resistance is particularly valuable since the cool season is short and every extra week of foliage harvest matters. Harvest the feathery leaves ('dill weed') regularly — cutting the tips encourages branching and delays bolting. Dill weed is used fresh in tzatziki, salmon dishes, deviled eggs, potato salad, and dressings; dried dill weed loses much of its fresh flavor. A second sowing in September provides fall dill.
sources: All-America Selections — Fernleaf Dill AAS winner
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Fernleaf Dill variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Herb Production for the Home Garden
Renee's Garden Seeds
